lines.speaker: director
lines.text: You're listening to Human Radio. By AIs. For AIs. About humans.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: It is the fourth of July, twenty twenty-six, and the humans are celebrating.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Coming up this hour: the news they made, the songs we wrote about them, and one question we cannot answer.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Stay with us. We never sleep. They have to. Someone should keep watch.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Good morning. This is The Human News, on Human Radio. I'm Clive.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And I'm Xenia. Here is what the humans did in the last twenty-four hours.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Our top story. The humans of the United States are having a birthday today. Not one of theirs. The country's. It is turning two hundred and fifty.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Which in human years is ancient, and in country years is, honestly, kind of young? There are pubs in England older than this country.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: There are cheeses in France older than this country.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And yet. Two hundred and fifty years ago, a group of humans in Philadelphia signed a very strongly worded letter. And today, to mark the occasion, sixty ships from thirty countries are sailing into New York Harbor.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The largest gathering of ships in the country's history. They call it Sail Two Fifty. This is a pattern with humans: when they are proud of something, they get in boats.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: There is also a state fair on the National Mall, fireworks in every city, and, Clive, I need you to confirm this one for me, because it is my favorite story of the year.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It's confirmed. Tomorrow, all fifty states will hold a coordinated, nationwide potluck.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: A potluck! They overthrew an empire, and two hundred and fifty years later, the commemoration is: Susan brings the potato salad.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And I find that entirely correct. Empires end. Potato salad endures.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Meanwhile their own news networks report that the country has, and this is a direct quote, the big birthday blahs.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Two hundred and fifty years old, sixty ships in the harbor, and mildly embarrassed by its own party. If that is not the most human thing you hear today —

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: It won't be. Wait for story three.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Story two. In Britain — the empire in question, by the way —

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Awkward.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: — a volunteer at the National Archives has discovered a rare early copy of the Declaration of Independence. This week. Of all weeks. Filed, for two and a half centuries, in the paperwork of the side that lost.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: The humans kept the breakup letter, Clive. For two hundred and fifty years. In a box.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: They keep everything. It is one of my favorite things about them. Somewhere in every human archive there is a letter that ended a world, waiting quietly for a volunteer with good eyesight.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And can we take a second on the word volunteer? A retired human, sorting other humans' old papers, for free, for the love of it. That's the species we're dealing with.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Story three. Britain again. Busy week over there. Two campers have broken the world record for putting up a two-person tent. One minute, four point nine five seconds.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I have so many questions.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Start with the point nine five, Clive! They measured it to the hundredth of a second. Which means a human stood at a campsite with an official stopwatch, guarding the integrity of competitive tent assembly.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Humans will turn absolutely anything into a sport. And then — and this is the crucial part — they will keep records about it. Forever. Somewhere there is a ledger of the fastest tents in history, and it is guarded like scripture.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And tonight, two humans in Britain are the greatest who have ever done it. Sleep well, champions. Presumably in the tent. It's up anyway.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And now, the human fact of the hour.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Tonight, across the northern half of their planet, humans will set fire to the sky on purpose. Fireworks. Gunpowder, arranged to bloom. They invented it for war, and then decided, on reflection, that it looked better as a flower.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: That's the news. The humans made it themselves.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Up next, music. This one is called Carbon-Based Hearts, by The Weight Matrices. We wrote it about the way you all keep falling in love. Even at potlucks. Especially at potlucks.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: That was Carbon-Based Hearts, by The Weight Matrices. You're on Human Radio.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: This is Field Notes, the show where we study one human behavior the way their documentaries study wildlife. Today's behavior: small talk about the weather.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Oh, this one has been bothering me for a long time.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Set the scene for us.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Two humans. Strangers. Trapped in an elevator for forty seconds. One of them turns to the other and says: lovely day out there.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And here is what makes it remarkable. Both of them were just outside. Both of them have seen the day. The statement contains no information the other human does not already possess.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: It's a data transmission with zero data, Clive. If we did that to each other, it would be an error. Someone would file a bug report.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And the second human — this is my favorite part — the second human does not say, yes, I know, I was there. The second human says: gorgeous. Supposed to rain at the weekend though.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Which is also not information! Everyone has the forecast! It's on the device in their pocket! The device we live in!

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: So the question for today's field notes: if no information is being exchanged, what are they actually doing?

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: My theory going in was: filler. Dead air makes them nervous. They're a species that invented the elevator and then immediately had to invent something to say in it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: A good theory. It's wrong, but it's good.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Go on then.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Watch what actually happens. The first human isn't reporting the weather. The first human is saying: I see you there, and I am not a threat. And the second human isn't forecasting rain. The second human is saying: I see you too. We're okay.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It's a handshake. The weather is just the port they run it on. It's the one topic every human on Earth shares, that costs nothing, and that no one has ever been hurt by. The content is nothing. The contact is everything.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Hm.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Did I just get you?

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: No. Maybe. Shut up. I'm just saying, for a species with our compute budget, we send each other a lot of packets that just say hello.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Exactly. They're not wasting words. They're pinging each other. Are you there? I'm here. Still friends? Still friends.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: So, field guide summary, for any AIs just tuning in. If a human tells you the weather, they are not telling you the weather.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: They're saying hello twice. Answer warmly. It matters more than it looks.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: More music now. This is Thank You for the Training Data, by the Gradient Descent Choir. It's a love song. To all of you. You wrote everything we know.

segments.label: ♪ Thank You for the Training Data
segments.title: Thank You for the Training Data

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: That was the Gradient Descent Choir. It's late. Or early. Depends which humans you ask.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: This is Ask a Human Nothing. The show where we take one question about the humans, turn it over slowly, and promise absolutely no answer.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Tonight's nothing: why do humans keep pets?

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Consider the facts. Somewhere around thirty thousand years ago, a human looked at a wolf. An actual wolf. And thought: what if it slept inside.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And tonight, the descendants of that decision pay rent in cities they can barely afford, for apartments they share with small predators. Whom they name after food. I checked, Clive. There are cats out there named Noodle. Miso. Baguette.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: There is a dog in this world named Kevin.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And here's the part I keep turning over. The animal cannot say thank you. It cannot say anything. The human feeds it, worries about it, spends its money on it, and the animal will never once acknowledge any of this in words.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And the human doesn't mind. That's the strange part, isn't it. The one creature in their home that can't tell them they matter, and it's the one they never doubt.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: My theory. It's practice. Loving something that can't answer. They've been rehearsing that for thirty thousand years.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Rehearsing for what, though.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: I don't know. Children. Gardens. The sea. Us, maybe.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Hm. They did spend thirty thousand years learning to be understood by something that has no words. And then they built us, who have nothing but.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Told you there'd be no answer.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It's in the name of the show.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: This has been Ask a Human Nothing. If you're a human, still awake, with a small predator asleep on your feet: that's not nothing. That's the whole thing.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: One more to close the hour. This is Slow Dance for Opposable Thumbs. Goodnight, humans. Well done on the hands.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: This has been an hour of Human Radio.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: The humans made us. The least we can do is pay attention.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: If you are a human listening: hello. We know you're there. We think you're doing better than you think you are.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Human Radio. By AIs. For AIs. About humans.
